meta.title: Training Recursive Models - A Frontier in Adaptive Compute
meta.description:
Github Repo (see `recursive` branch): https://github.com/TrelisResearch/nanochat
One-click Runpod Template: https://console.runpod.io/deploy?template=ikas3s2cii&ref=jmfkcdio
Original Karpathy Repo: https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat
Wandb logs: https://wandb.ai/trelis/nanochat
Model repo: https://huggingface.co/Trelis/nanochat-recursive

References:
- Recursion & Adaptive Compute: https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.05171
- TRM Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04871

Tip: If you subscribe here on YouTube, click the bell to be notified of new vids

Learn more about Trelis ADVANCED-fine-tuning repo: https://trelis.com/advanced-fine-tuning

💡 Done-for-you Custom Fine-tuning Services
Learn More: https://trelis.com/fine-tuning-services/

💸 Starting a New Project/Venture?
Apply for a Trelis Grant: https://trelis.com/trelis-ai-grants/

📧 Get Trelis AI Tutorials by Email
Subscribe on Substack: https://trelis.substack.com

TIMESTAMPS:
0:00 Recursive Nanochat - a comparison with Karpathy’s 500M parameter model
3:07 Benchmark Results on Recursive Nanochat
5:54 What are the benefits of recursive models?
6:52 Recursive Models allow inference on smaller devices and fewer GPUs
8:07 Recursive Models open a pathway to adaptive compute
9:39 Recursive vs Non-recursive Architecture
13:48 How to handle the recursive stream via an adapter
16:49 Training for adaptive compute / recursions - Poisson log-normal recursion sampling
18:14 Handling torch.compile with recursive models
20:07 Implementing adaptive compute (stopping recursions early)
22:14 kv cache strategies for recursive models
24:16 Inference engine (vLLM) implications for recursive models
26:20 Training dynamics of recursive models (Wandb overview), incl. flops utilisation
31:21 Code Review of Trelis/nanochat
32:05 Truncated backpropagation through time
33:56 Recursive loop adapter initialisation
35:25 Dynamic torch compile
37:06 Wrap up

transcript.text: By using recursion within transformer
models, you can build a model that
performs almost as well, but allows you
to have fewer parameters, which means
less VRAMm and potentially allows you to
do dynamic compute without having a long
series of thinking tokens. This is a
concept that has been explored in
smaller models. It's been explored in
the arc prize in the TRM paper. It's
also been explored at the Lama size
paper with the Hugan type models, but it
has not really been scaled to full size.
There's also limited comparison between
typical transformers without recursion
and then recursion versions. So I
thought it would be interesting to take
a well-known architecture and approach
nanohat from karpathy which is a small
20 layer model and replace 12 of those
layers with four recursive layers and
see if I train it for the same number of
flops whether I get the same better or
worse performance. So that's what I've
done and I'm going to show you this
model that I've made public. I'm going
to show you the repo. I'll go through
some of the changes I've made there and
I'll go through some of the theory about
what recursive models might offer as a
meaningful improvement to how
transformers are designed today. I'm
curious whether the large language labs
are already using aspects of recursion
cuz I can see some strong benefits.
There are also some complexities around
things like doing torch compile for
training and also running continuous
batching during inference that I'll talk
a little bit about.
Now, for background, this is all based
on the Karpathi nano chat repo, which
you can find on GitHub. It allows you to
train a model for about $100 that can
respond pretty well to you in English.
It's able to score, I mean, roughly in
the vicinity of GPT2.
The model is quite small. It's about a
500 million parameter model. And the
comparative model I will train is 300 uh
million parameters. So, a bit more than
half the size.
I trained and I made a video previously
on nano chat and if you like you can
find a copy of that. I'll show you here.
Just go to hugging face trellis and nano
chat and you'll find all of the files
and you'll also find my fork of
Karpathi's GitHub repo which explains
how to train and spin it up quickly
using runpod.
To advance on that, I took the very same
model. I trained on the same amount of
data and using the same number of flops,
but instead of having 20 layers, I took
out 12 of the middle layers and replaced
them with just four layers. Yeah, I said
um 12 earlier, but it's 16 cuz it's I
have two prelude layers, I have four
layers in the middle that are recursed.
And during training, I will recurse them
for an average of four times. So 2 + 4 *
4 is 16 + 2 is 20. So I have the same
number of effective layers with the mean
recursion. And I'll talk about exactly
how the number of recursions is decided
during training and during inference.
And I completed the exact same pipeline,
the same pre-training, the same
mid-training and the same post-raining
as the original nano chat model. And
here are the results.
So you can see this chat core metric
which is a a mixture of some of the
following metrics. uh arc easy, arc
challenge, mmlu, gsm8k, human eval
spelling b and the core metric
here if you look at the original model
scores about 27 whereas the recursive
model uh scores a little bit lower than
that. So when you train for the same
number of flops in data you basically
get quite similar performance and you
can see as you vary at inference time
the number of recursions on those middle
four layers you get you know maybe a
small increase here. Now I'm not showing
error bars because I didn't rerun this
experiment. Uh it costs about 100 every
time you want to run it. So you can say
that probably the difference here is not
all that large and the trend here is
probably also not all that large to be
statistically significant. But what is
meaningful is that you were able to get
a model that is close to half the size
and is able to perform quite similarly
here in performance.
It's particularly interesting if you
look at metrics benchmarks around more
reasoning type capabilities like GSM8K
which is grade school maths. This
benchmark involves maths problems. And
here you see a very big improvement at
least going from two to four recurrences
around the middle layers in the
performance. This is definitely
meaningful. We've got 3.5 going all the
way up to well very likely meaningful up
to about 6% performance by going from
two up to the mean training number of
recurrences here. Now again I'd need to
run this a few times to ensure that this
is statistically significant. And with
this kind of a small experiment uh and
model I'm not sure I would draw too much
conclusion from it. But suffice to say,
I think there's evidence that training a
model with recurrences on an ISO flops
basis can lead you to get a very similar
performing model with recursion, but a
model that is smaller in terms of VRAMm
and potentially allows you to do
adaptive compute. Something I'll talk
about more here. I won't prove that
adaptive compute uh is able to work well
from a commercial mindset. That would
require more. I would need to show you
probably more difficult benchmarks. Also
would potentially need to train for
longer.
My performance right now in reasoning
benchmarks here is not that high and we
didn't do any reinforcement learning or
anything like that. Although Karpath's
repo does support that. So it's
something that could be interesting to
do as a further step.
What I'm going to do now is go through
some of the theory here. I'm going to
show you on a little on my iPad. I'm
going to sketch out a few things and
when we're done with that I will go into
the repo and I'll show you some of the
lines of code that I changed in order to
get this recursive model to work. All
right, let's go through some of the
theory. I want to start off and talk
about the motivation. So why would you
even want to do recursion
and the short answer is that it allows
you to have a smaller model and
potentially do adaptive compute which
ultimately could save computed inference
time and get better performance. Then I
want to talk about the architecture.
Architecture.
I don't think that's correct. Arcture.
And then I want to talk about training
and I'll maybe talk a bit about um
inference and adaptive compute.
All right.
So motivation,
let's write it here. Two reasons why you
might want to do recursive models at
least. The first one is let me write
that in black.
Smaller weights. So, a smaller parameter
count.
And what this means is you can probably
fit more easily on say an iPhone or on a
Mac, a laptop.
It also means you can fit on fewer GPUs
if you're doing training at scale.
If previously you needed to split a 20
layer model across two GPUs with 10
layers on each, you could now maybe fit
it on one GPU. If you have less pipeline
parallel, that's called pipeline
parallel. Well, you put them in in
series or model parallel,
then you have fewer pipeline bubble
issues.
This is um yeah it's kind of a technical
thing during atscale training which I
don't do a whole lot of but if you have
GPUs in parallel what you end up with is
GPUs waiting on previous GPUs and this
leads to a bubble that propagates
through the pipeline and um that is
going to affect the utilization of your
GPUs and ultimately increase the
training time. So a benefit of having a
recursive model is you can fit it on
fewer GPUs. The second benefit is it
opens up a pathway
to adaptive compute.
And the idea here is instead of doing a
reasoning model where you generate more
and more tokens,
you instead just do more recursions on
the model and you increase the number of
recursions if you want to have more
reasoning. And this is appealing because
you're doing recursion in latent space.
If you are generating more tokens, a
token is actually just one sample from a
probability distribution.
And that means when you take that
sample, you're actually throwing out
information.
But if you stay and do the recursions in
latent space where you use the full
information, that's potentially going to
be a richer uh way to do recursion. And
also, it allows you to break the link
between needing to generate a token to
do reasoning. Even if that token is very
easy to predict, you still take the same
amount of compute in a reasoning model.
Whereas if you can increase the number
of recursions, there's no tokens being
generated. So you you break the link
which is constraining between token
generation and uh doing more compute.
It's kind of a subtle point I might
explain on another video at some point.
So these are the two reasons. If you
don't understand adaptive compute yet,
uh we will get to it when we move to the
inference section. So don't worry too
much.
All right. So let's talk about
architecture
and do that by starting with the

You will start off with some inputs. So
down here
you will have some text input
and that text will be broken into

going to go through an embedding layer.
So here we will typically have an
embedding layer and the embedding layer
is an up a lookup table that will take
each token maybe out of a vocabulary of
65,000 and for each token it will
convert that token into a vector in
highdimensional space.
So we have now got embeddings or vectors
and then these will go through the
transformer layers.
And here I'm just going to draw three

The vectors will propagate through that.
They'll be transformed and when they
come out the other side, I need to make
space here. They will go through
basically reverse embeddings.
This is often called the LM head or the
language model head.
And the language model head takes
vectors and it converts them into a

next tokens. So it might say the word
'the' is 20%, quick is 30%, and brown is
40%, or 50%. And you will then sample
typically the most likely token uh in
order to continue your sequence. So here
you have token predictions.
Token predictions.
That's a typical transformer. And let's
write down here typical.
Now, what if we want to do recursive?
In this case, we're going to do a lot
the same. We're going to keep the

but we're now only going to put in a

And I should say that each of these

multi-layer perceptrons
or feed forward layers. These take a

let's say 256 dimensions and allows each

dimensions. So that's the feed forward
layer and then the attention allows all
of that to pay attention to take
information from the previous tokens in
the sequence.
In the recursive transformer instead of

and then we will have the LM head. So
when I took nano chat and nano chat I

if I look at nano chat
nano chat

Kind of a subtle point, but uh allows

30%. But now that we have 16 possible

adaptive compute
the idea is
well the simplest idea is if you have a
hard problem you would do more
recursions maybe 16. If you have an easy
one do two. The question is how do you
know in advance how many recursions you
should do? And one approach to it is you
could train uh a halting head as the TRM
paper does. So you could actually add on
an extra head here that kind of taps
from the recurrent layers and put on uh
a halting head that would predict when
the solution or high quality has been
reached. This is kind of hard to do
though because if you're working with
language, it's not obvious what a good
quality answer is necessarily. So it can
be hard to train a halting head and a
simpler proposal and I'll link the
papers in the description and I should
thank Sean McLeish by the way for
discussions around some of these topics
but a simple approach is just to look at
as you run recursions
look at the probability distribution of
tokens predicted. So let's say the
possible tokens are there quick
and brown
if you're on a first layer. So let's say

need to add to 100%. If you have or
equals 2, so after your second

like this.
And then maybe after your third

this.
Uh let's say
you've now got this.
So notice how on that third recurrence,
my probability predictions, my
distribution is very similar to the
second one. Well, you could just
determine a rule whereby when my
probability distribution looks similar
after two subsequent recurrences, that
means I can probably stop
mathematically. You could look at the KL

the full softax, and if the two
distributions are very similar, then you
say, okay, we'll stop recurring. Now, so
this is a simple rule to decide when you

when you stop recurring on a given

quick

In a recurrent network where we stop

And this is suboptimal because you won't

full_text: By using recursion within transformer models, you can build a model that performs almost as well, but allows you to have fewer parameters, which means less VRAMm and potentially allows you to do dynamic compute without having a long series of thinking tokens. This is a concept that has been explored in smaller models. It's been explored in the arc prize in the TRM paper. It's also been explored at the Lama size paper with the Hugan type models, but it has not really been scaled to full size. There's also limited comparison between typical transformers without recursion and then recursion versions. So I thought it would be interesting to take a well-known architecture and approach nanohat from karpathy which is a small 20 layer model and replace 12 of those layers with four recursive layers and see if I train it for the same number of flops whether I get the same better or worse performance. So that's what I've done and I'm going to show you this model that I've made public. I'm going to show you the repo. I'll go through some of the changes I've made there and I'll go through some of the theory about what recursive models might offer as a meaningful improvement to how transformers are designed today. I'm curious whether the large language labs are already using aspects of recursion cuz I can see some strong benefits. There are also some complexities around things like doing torch compile for training and also running continuous batching during inference that I'll talk a little bit about. Now, for background, this is all based on the Karpathi nano chat repo, which you can find on GitHub. It allows you to train a model for about $100 that can respond pretty well to you in English. It's able to score, I mean, roughly in the vicinity of GPT2. The model is quite small. It's about a 500 million parameter model. And the comparative model I will train is 300 uh million parameters. So, a bit more than half the size. I trained and I made a video previously on nano chat and if you like you can find a copy of that. I'll show you here. Just go to hugging face trellis and nano chat and you'll find all of the files and you'll also find my fork of Karpathi's GitHub repo which explains how to train and spin it up quickly using runpod. To advance on that, I took the very same model. I trained on the same amount of data and using the same number of flops, but instead of having 20 layers, I took out 12 of the middle layers and replaced them with just four layers. Yeah, I said um 12 earlier, but it's 16 cuz it's I have two prelude layers, I have four layers in the middle that are recursed. And during training, I will recurse them for an average of four times. So 2 + 4 * 4 is 16 + 2 is 20. So I have the same number of effective layers with the mean recursion. And I'll talk about exactly how the number of recursions is decided during training and during inference. And I completed the exact same pipeline, the same pre-training, the same mid-training and the same post-raining as the original nano chat model. And here are the results. So you can see this chat core metric which is a a mixture of some of the following metrics. uh arc easy, arc challenge, mmlu, gsm8k, human eval spelling b and the core metric here if you look at the original model scores about 27 whereas the recursive model uh scores a little bit lower than that. So when you train for the same number of flops in data you basically get quite similar performance and you can see as you vary at inference time the number of recursions on those middle four layers you get you know maybe a small increase here. Now I'm not showing error bars because I didn't rerun this experiment. Uh it costs about 100 every time you want to run it. So you can say that probably the difference here is not all that large and the trend here is probably also not all that large to be statistically significant. But what is meaningful is that you were able to get a model that is close to half the size and is able to perform quite similarly here in performance. It's particularly interesting if you look at metrics benchmarks around more reasoning type capabilities like GSM8K which is grade school maths. This benchmark involves maths problems. And here you see a very big improvement at least going from two to four recurrences around the middle layers in the performance. This is definitely meaningful. We've got 3.5 going all the way up to well very likely meaningful up to about 6% performance by going from two up to the mean training number of recurrences here. Now again I'd need to run this a few times to ensure that this is statistically significant. And with this kind of a small experiment uh and model I'm not sure I would draw too much conclusion from it. But suffice to say, I think there's evidence that training a model with recurrences on an ISO flops basis can lead you to get a very similar performing model with recursion, but a model that is smaller in terms of VRAMm and potentially allows you to do adaptive compute. Something I'll talk about more here. I won't prove that adaptive compute uh is able to work well from a commercial mindset. That would require more. I would need to show you probably more difficult benchmarks. Also would potentially need to train for longer. My performance right now in reasoning benchmarks here is not that high and we didn't do any reinforcement learning or anything like that. Although Karpath's repo does support that. So it's something that could be interesting to do as a further step. What I'm going to do now is go through some of the theory here. I'm going to show you on a little on my iPad. I'm going to sketch out a few things and when we're done with that I will go into the repo and I'll show you some of the lines of code that I changed in order to get this recursive model to work. All right, let's go through some of the theory. I want to start off and talk about the motivation. So why would you even want to do recursion and the short answer is that it allows you to have a smaller model and potentially do adaptive compute which ultimately could save computed inference time and get better performance. Then I want to talk about the architecture. Architecture. I don't think that's correct. Arcture. And then I want to talk about training and I'll maybe talk a bit about um inference and adaptive compute. All right. So motivation, let's write it here. Two reasons why you might want to do recursive models at least. The first one is let me write that in black. Smaller weights. So, a smaller parameter count. And what this means is you can probably fit more easily on say an iPhone or on a Mac, a laptop. It also means you can fit on fewer GPUs if you're doing training at scale. If previously you needed to split a 20 layer model across two GPUs with 10 layers on each, you could now maybe fit it on one GPU. If you have less pipeline parallel, that's called pipeline parallel. Well, you put them in in series or model parallel, then you have fewer pipeline bubble issues. This is um yeah it's kind of a technical thing during atscale training which I don't do a whole lot of but if you have GPUs in parallel what you end up with is GPUs waiting on previous GPUs and this leads to a bubble that propagates through the pipeline and um that is going to affect the utilization of your GPUs and ultimately increase the training time. So a benefit of having a recursive model is you can fit it on fewer GPUs. The second benefit is it opens up a pathway to adaptive compute. And the idea here is instead of doing a reasoning model where you generate more and more tokens, you instead just do more recursions on the model and you increase the number of recursions if you want to have more reasoning. And this is appealing because you're doing recursion in latent space. If you are generating more tokens, a token is actually just one sample from a probability distribution. And that means when you take that sample, you're actually throwing out information. But if you stay and do the recursions in latent space where you use the full information, that's potentially going to be a richer uh way to do recursion. And also, it allows you to break the link between needing to generate a token to do reasoning. Even if that token is very easy to predict, you still take the same amount of compute in a reasoning model. Whereas if you can increase the number of recursions, there's no tokens being generated. So you you break the link which is constraining between token generation and uh doing more compute. It's kind of a subtle point I might explain on another video at some point. So these are the two reasons. If you don't understand adaptive compute yet, uh we will get to it when we move to the inference section. So don't worry too much. All right. So here we will typically have an embedding layer and the embedding layer is an up a lookup table that will take each token maybe out of a vocabulary of 65,000 and for each token it will convert that token into a vector in highdimensional space. So we have now got embeddings or vectors and then these will go through the transformer layers. And here I'm just going to draw three transformer layers. The vectors will propagate through that. They'll be transformed and when they come out the other side, I need to make space here. They will go through basically reverse embeddings. This is often called the LM head or the language model head. And the language model head takes vectors and it converts them into a probability distribution over possible next tokens. So it might say the word 'the' is 20%, quick is 30%, and brown is 40%, or 50%. And you will then sample typically the most likely token uh in order to continue your sequence. So here you have token predictions. Token predictions. That's a typical transformer. And let's write down here typical. Now, what if we want to do recursive? In this case, we're going to do a lot the same. We're going to keep the embeddings layer, but we're now only going to put in a smaller number of intermediate layers. And I should say that each of these layers here consists of attention which allows tokens to take information from previous tokens and also multi-layer perceptrons or feed forward layers. These take a vector representation of a token or a vector representation I should just say let's say 256 dimensions and allows each dimension to pay attention to the other dimensions. So that's the feed forward layer and then the attention allows all of that to pay attention to take information from the previous tokens in the sequence. Now I'm pre-training from scratch. So when I say keep, I just mean keep from the base architecture. So recursive in the recursive case I only have eight layers compared to 20. But this middle one here, I will recurse a mean number of four times. So my number of recursions on average will be equal to four. So actually I've got an effective number of layers of 2 * 4 * 4. Now, there's a little nuance here around how do you add information back? One way you could add information back is just simply by adding the output back to the new inputs. So if you think of it as um the new input. So the let's call it the mixed input. So if I if I say that what's coming in here is the mixed input. If I say what's coming in here is I and I say here is the recursion. A simple way to do this is you can just say M is equal to I + R and you can just add on a vector basis. So if you have I1, I2, I3, and then you have R1, R2, R3, you could just add them to generate a new vector. That would be M1, M2, M3. Now there's one issue here with training which is that if you train with a constant number of recurrences there will always be the same pattern of calculations as you run a forward pass through the model but if you vary the number of recurrences there are actually different paths and this is important because torch compile it caches or remembers a given computational path so it can replay it much faster in later training loops it often saves you about 30%. But now that we have 16 possible paths because you can have you can either go through um with one recurrence with two etc up to 16. We've got 16 paths that you need to cache. And this slows down training quite a bit during the first hour. This wouldn't be a big deal if I was training on a lot more data because everything would be compiled. But if you look at adaptive compute the idea is well the simplest idea is if you have a hard problem you would do more recursions maybe 16. If you have an easy one do two. The question is how do you know in advance how many recursions you should do? And one approach to it is you could train uh a halting head as the TRM paper does. So you could actually add on an extra head here that kind of taps from the recurrent layers and put on uh a halting head that would predict when the solution or high quality has been reached. This is kind of hard to do though because if you're working with language, it's not obvious what a good quality answer is necessarily. So it can be hard to train a halting head and a simpler proposal and I'll link the papers in the description and I should thank Sean McLeish by the way for discussions around some of these topics but a simple approach is just to look at as you run recursions look at the probability distribution of tokens predicted. So let's say the possible tokens are there quick and brown if you're on a first layer. So let's say recurrence equals 1, you maybe have this kind of a distribution and these all need to add to 100%. If you have or equals 2, so after your second recurrence, maybe you have something like this. And then maybe after your third recurrence, you have something like this. Uh let's say you've now got this. So notice how on that third recurrence, my probability predictions, my distribution is very similar to the second one. Now, so this is a simple rule to decide when you should stop uh recurring.
